revision.text:
{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue ware]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue ware in western Japan, and the Sue ware from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue ware is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji ware]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue ware shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:|Sabukaze Remains}} *[https://www.city.setouchi.lg.jp/soshiki/23/2297.html Setouchi city Official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.sabukaze.com/heritage.html Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan Information official site] {{in lang|ja}}

The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue ware]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue ware in western Japan, and the Sue ware from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue ware is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji ware]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue ware shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]

{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue ware]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue ware in western Japan, and the Sue ware from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue ware is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji ware]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue ware shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:|Sabukaze Remains}} *[https://www.city.setouchi.lg.jp/soshiki/23/2297.html Setouchi city Official home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.sabukaze.com/heritage.html Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan Information official site] {{in lang|ja}}

{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue ware]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue ware in western Japan, and the Sue ware from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue ware is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji ware]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue ware shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]

{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue ware]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue ware in western Japan, and the Sue ware from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue ware is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji ware]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue ware shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]

{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue ware]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue ware in western Japan, and the Sue ware from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue ware is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji ware]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue ware shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]

{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue ware]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue ware in western Japan, and the Sue ware from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue ware is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji ware]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue ware shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]
==References== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue ware]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue ware in western Japan, and the Sue ware from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue ware is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji ware]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue ware shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]
==References== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue ware]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue ware in western Japan, and the Sue ware from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue ware is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji ware]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue ware shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]
==References== {{reflist}}

{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue ware]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue ware in western Japan, and the Sue ware from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue ware is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji ware]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue ware shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]

{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue ware]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue ware in western Japan, and the Sue ware from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue ware is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji ware]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue ware shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue ware items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue pottery]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue pottery in western Japan, and the Sue pottery from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue pottery is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji pottery]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue pottery shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue pottery items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]
==References== {{reflist}}

{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue pottery]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue pottery in western Japan, and the Sue pottery from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue pottery is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji pottery]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue pottery shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue pottery items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]

{{Short description|Remains of pottery kilns in Setouchi, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Sabukaze kiln ruins | native_name = 寒風古窯跡群 | native_name_lang = | alternate_name = | image =Sabukaze 02.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Sabukaze kiln ruins | map_size = 270px | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|region:JP-33|display=inline}} | location = [[Setouchi, Okayama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'yō region]] | type = | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs = [[Asuka period|Asuka]] - [[Heian period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|39|16.4|N|134|8|14.0|E|}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Sabukaze kiln ruins'''|寒風古窯跡群| Sabukaze koyōseki-gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of the remains of [[kiln]]s for firing [[Sue pottery]] pottery from the start of the [[Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]] located in the [[Ushimado, Okayama|Ushimado]] neighborhood of the city of [[Setouchi, Okayama|Setouchi]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] in the [[San'yō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was the largest production area of Sue pottery in western Japan, and the Sue pottery from these kilns evolved into the current [[Bizen ware]].<ref name="bizen-history">{{cite web|url=http://www.explorejapaneseceramics.com/towns/bizen/bizen-history.html|title=Bizen – History |publisher= Explore Japanese Ceramics|author=Bizen Ware Ceramics Friendship Society|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref><ref name="touyuukai history">{{cite web|url=http://www.touyuukai.jp/english/history.htm|title=History of Bizen ware|publisher=Kyōdō Kumiai Okayama-ken Bizenyaki Tōyūkai|first=John Thomas|last=Wells|access-date=October 13, 2016}}</ref> The site has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 1986.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139244|title=寒風古窯跡群|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== Sue pottery is pottery brought to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] in the middle of the Kofun period (approximately 1600 years ago). New techniques are used that were not used in [[Yayoi pottery]] and [[Haji pottery]], which were continuations from [[Jomon pottery]], including the use of the [[potter's wheel]] and semi-underground ''[[anagama]]''-style kilns. By using firing temperatures in excess of 1000 deg C, [[Redox|reduction flame]] firing became possible, resulting in a hard blue-grey pottery that had less porosity than previous techniques.
At the beginning of the twentieth century a local historian, Tokizane Mokusui, collected a large amount of Sue pottery shards from the vicinity of the ruins and published a research journal, which led to recognition of the existence of the ruins in academia. From 1978, [[archaeological excavation]]s were carried out, and continued from 2005 to 2008 to preserve and open the ruins to the public. The ruins consist of three kiln sites: Kiln No. 1, Kiln No. 2, and Kiln No. 3, however, multiple ''[[noborigama]]''-style kilns with lengths of over ten meters have been found at each location. Three kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 1 kiln, and currently, a total of five kiln sites have been confirmed at the No. 3 kiln. The kilns are located on the southwestern slope of a hill with an elevation of 50 to 60 meters, and the site also contains the ruins of a building thought to be the remains of a workshop and a pond. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> There is also a ''[[kofun]]'' burial mount located on the south slope of the hill, but this is not thought to be related to the kilns.
Along with the ashes of firewood, unsuccessfully fired pottery and other items have been unearthed. There items indicate that in addition to jars, plates, and bottles, the kilns also produced items such as ceramic coffins, [[Shibi (roof tile)|''Shibi'' roof ornaments]] and inkstones. As Sue pottery items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]

As Sue pottery items from these kilns have also unearthed from the capitals of [[Heijō-kyō]] and [[Fujiwara-kyō]] in [[Nara Prefecture]], this suggests that the kiln was not simply a local kiln, but had an official function as well. The kiln was most active for a one-hundred year period from the beginning of the 7th century to the beginning of the 8th century. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The unearthed Sue pottery and other artifacts are stored and exhibited at the {{nihongo|'''Sabukaze Tōgei Kaikan'''|寒風陶芸会館}} on site<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Okayama)]] *[[Oibora-Asakura Sue Ware Kiln Site]] *[[Dodo Sue Ware Kiln ruins]]
